entry.title: Managing PFAS with SAP: Unlock Your Data Treasures
entry.content:
If you run SAP ERP and want to tackle PFAS, we have excellent news: your SAP system offers a wealth of treasures – data and information - that you can use:  

  • It records what you purchase, produce, store, and sell.
  • It knows which components in what quantities make up your products.
  • It tells you what you buy from whom.  

Discover how to unlock SAP data using an integrated and sustainable approach to handle PFAS. For insights into current PFAS regulations and their implications for your business, don't miss our initial article about PFAS Regulations and Business Challenges in the series Managing PFAS with SAP. 

SAP’s Answer to PFAS Business Challenges

Utilizing the right resources in your SAP system guarantees progress in tackling the following core business challenge:

Maintaining records of PFAS compliance efforts.This facilitates regulatory audits and ensures adherence to legal obligations.
Identification of PFAS-containing materials throughout the supply chain.This facilitates risk assessment, substitution planning, and collaboration with suppliers.
Creation of a PFAS inventory.This facilitates tracking and reporting of PFAS-related metrics and enhances transparency.

Your SAP system can support the entire product lifecycle. This includes tracking PFAS in products, monitoring PFAS exposure during the manufacturing process or managing PFAS waste. This blog post focuses only on the aspect of product compliance.

Access the Vault: The Wide Range of Available Functions in SAP

You don’t have to go treasure hunting on your own. SAP Product Compliance Applications do the job with you. They are perfectly tailored to manage the required PFAS information on components and ingredients all the way down to the respective chemical substance or CAS number level. To do so, they process existing data and information:

  • The logistics integration enables product compliance data management on a material level considering different suppliers and manufacturers:
    • The material master contains information on the materials you procure, produce, store, and sell.
    • The bill of material (BOM) is a list of parts, items, assemblies and other components required to create your product.
    • The vendor master data stores the vendors that supply your company.
    • The purchasing info records connect purchased materials with vendors.
    • The source lists and approved manufacturer part lists specify the allowed sources of a (manufacturer part) material for a certain plant and period.
  • The possibility to connect to existing business processes of other areas such as sales and distribution, purchasing, manufacturing, and product development, facilitates the cross-functional availability and reporting of compliance information.

Make Use of the Vault: SAP Product Compliance Applications

Over the years, SAP’s product compliance solutions evolved, changed, and expanded.

Officially they are called SAP S/4HANA for product compliance, SAP Product and REACH Compliance, and SAP Product Safety.

Unofficially old terms like EHSM and EH&S have stuck around. Some habits are hard to break! Go deeper with our Overview and History of SAP Product Compliance Applications or browse the SAP Product Compliance Community.

The Central Logic

The core entity is the compliance data object (CDO) - like a material in the material master. The object stores everything: the compliance information, composition, supplier listings, relevant documents, and specifications.

The core challenge is reacting to changes. We are talking about new legal obligations like PFAS regulations, list updates like in REACH and RoHS, or product structure changes due to new suppliers and the redesign of your product. Reacting to any of those changes is crucial. You want to avoid a one-time exercise and instead aspire to a living system:

  • The Regulatory List Management handles all regulated substances for a specific regulation including possible exemptions.
  • The Release Management ensures reliability and documentation of compliance information.
  • The Change Management detects changes in the product structure and triggers the recalculation of the compliance status.
  • Interfaces and exchange formats enable reporting and electronic data exchange (e.g. IMDS, PSN, BOMcheck, IPC1752).

Realizing an Integrated PFAS Approach

PFAS is not a standalone topic. It makes sense to handle all product compliance topics like REACH-SVHCs, RoHS or PFAS in one integrated approach. SAP Product Compliance Applications are tailored to do just that. They execute checks against regulatory lists based on substances in your products including assemblies and supplier parts as defined in your BOMs.

OECD PFAS List And Other Regulatory Lists in Your SAP System:

 OECD PFAS Compliance Check for a Product in Your SAP System:

Remember, there is no IF, only a WHEN when it comes to PFAS regulations, substitutions, and phase-outs. Even if you are not affected now, you will be in the future. This means you may be confronted with not one, but several PFAS lists.

Bonus: Our strategic partner Yordas, an expert on chemical regulatory data, provides regulatory lists and PFAS substance identification without you having to stay on top of updated or new lists that might be published by the EPA, ECHA, or other authorities.

Extend the Vault: Product Compliance Collaboration

Recognizing the increasing amount of time and resources our customers are spending to fill compliance data gaps, we have developed opesus Product Compliance Collaboration (PCC).

opesus PCC is a central hub designed to support collaboration with suppliers, service providers, customers, authorities, and business units within your own company. It leverages your single source of truth with native integration into SAP product compliance applications. The tool is configurable for new upcoming reporting requirements.

The bottom line: For a topic as big as PFAS, it makes sense to start with what you already have. You can unlock your SAP ERP data treasury with SAP Product Compliance Applications to use existing information and stay on top of data changes. Stay tuned for our upcoming blog post in this series to discover how to gather missing compliance information with an integrated, highly automated, and auditable Product Compliance Collaboration solution.

entry.title: Part 2: Climate Risk Management - Methodology and Assumptions
entry.content:
Welcome back to Part 2 of our blog series where we (Ankit and Saeed) delve into the process of executing a climate risk scenario analysis within SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM). PaPM comes with a wide range of sample business scenarios that can be run out of the box. In this series, we aim to guide you through the using PaPM's Sample Content titled Climate Risk Scenario Analysis to assess the financial impacts of climate risks on various real estate assets and portfolios. The series will cover the methodology, data integration, and calculation steps of the sample content.


This blog series contains 3 parts:
• Part 1: Climate Risk Management Introduction.
• Part 2: Climate Risk Management - Methodology and Assumptions.
• Part 3: Financial KPIs and Calculation Steps.


In the first part of our blog series, we covered the impact of climate risks on economies through various channels, the importance of understanding the relationship between climate and the economy for informed financial decision-making, and the role of advanced methodologies and Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) in managing climate risks.
Part 2 of this blog will focus on the methodology and assumptions that form the foundation of the climate risk scenario analysis, which quantifies the impact of climate change on real estate investments from an investor's perspective.


Climate Data for Analysis


Climate variable data is crucial for accurately assessing the risks and impacts of climate change on various sectors. This data provides essential insights into potential environmental changes, helping stakeholders make informed decisions to mitigate financial and operational risks. The climate data used for this analysis is sourced from Climate Analytics, a global climate science and policy institute recommended by the Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS). While the sample content in this blog uses the data outlined below, PaPM is a flexible tool that can be adapted to work with the data you have available.


The following variables were considered in this model:

 

Variable NameDefinition
Mean Air TemperatureAir temperature refers to the temperature of air masses near the Earth's surface (2 metres above the ground in this case).
PrecipitationPrecipitation is defined as the mass of water (both rainfall and snowfall) falling on the Earth's surface, per unit area and time. The data used for this variable have undergone a bias-adjustment procedure to correct for deviations between modeled and observed values over the time period where they overlap.
Pressure AdjustedAtmospheric pressure quantifies the force that would be exerted by the weight of the column of air situated above a given location, per unit area.
Radiations AdjustedDownwelling longwave radiation is defined as the downward energy flux in the form of infrared light that reaches the Earth's surface.
Relative HumidityRelative humidity is defined as the ratio of water vapour in the air to the total amount that could be held at its current temperature (saturation level)
Specific HumiditySpecific humidity is defined as the mass of water vapour contained in each kg of air. Here we consider specific humidity at 2 metres above ground.
Snowfall
Snowfall is defined as the mass of water falling on the Earth's surface in the form of snow, per unit area and time.
Temperature Maximum AdjustedDaily maximum air temperature is defined as the peak air temperature reached in a day, in this case at 2 meters above the ground.

Temperature Minimum Adjusted

Daily minimum air temperature is defined as the lowest air temperature reached in a day, in this case at 2 meters above the ground
Wind Speed AdjustedVelocity of an air mass at 10 meters above ground.
One in Hundred Years Expected Damages from Tropical CyclonesDamage expected to occur once in 100 years, assuming constant GDP size and distribution as of 2005.
Annual Expected Damages from River FloodsAnnual expected damage from river floods, assuming constant GDP size and distribution as of 2005.
Annual Expected Damages from Tropical CyclonesAnnual expected damage from tropical cyclones, assuming constant GDP size and distribution as of 2005.
Annual Maximum River Flood DepthDepth during the most severe flood of the year.
Land Fraction Annually Exposed to HeatwavesPart of the population experiencing a heatwave annually, in a grid cell of 0.5° resolution.
Land Fraction Annually Exposed to River FloodsFraction of land area flooded during the annual maximum event.
Land Fraction Annually Exposed to WildfiresAnnual aggregate of land area burnt at least once a year by wildfires.

Table 1 - Definition of the variables used in modeling 

Analysis Methodology

Variables are clustered around specific perils to streamline the assessment of climate-related risks by grouping related factors that collectively influence each hazard, such as
floods or heatwaves. This approach enhances the accuracy of risk evaluation and allows for more targeted mitigation strategies, ensuring that peril-based products are effectively tailored to address the most relevant risks.
• Climate Change Variables : Relative Humidity, Specific Humidity, Precipitation, Snowfall, Atmospheric Pressure, Radiations Adjusted, Wind Speed Adjusted, Mean Air Temperature, Daily Maximum Air Temperature, and Daily Minimum Air Temperature.
• Wind : Changes in Wind Speed and Pressure.
• Precipitation : Precipitation, Snowfall, Relative Humidity, and Specific Humidity.
• Cyclones : One in Hundred Years Expected Damages and Annual Expected Damages from Tropical Cyclones.
• Heatwaves : Annual Exposure to Heatwaves.
• Floods : Annual Expected Damages from Floods and Land Fraction Exposed to River Floods.
• Wildfires : Land Fraction Annually Exposed to Wildfires.

With the climatic variables effectively clustered around key perils, we can now move on to the critical step of calculating the potential financial impacts. This calculation will help translate the identified risks into actionable insights, enabling a clear understanding of how climate change could affect asset values and financial performance.

Climate Delta Risk Calculation Methodology

Climate Value at Risk (CVaR) is the primary financial metric used in this Sample Content to quantify the potential financial losses that could result from climate-related risks. It works by estimating the expected costs associated with climate impacts on an asset, portfolio, or company, and comparing these costs to the total value of the underlying asset.
To calculate CVaR, we first estimate costs due to chronic and acute risks for eight scenarios. Both types of risk are considered because chronic risks represent the long-term, gradual impacts of climate change on assets, while acute risks capture the immediate, severe consequences of extreme events. Together, they provide a comprehensive view of potential vulnerabilities, ensuring more robust financial and strategic planning.

Chronic Risks:

Chronic risks are associated with long-term impacts like asset damage and business interruption. The costs associated with these risks are modeled using:

 

Cost = number of exceedances × vulnerability × vulnerability reduction × optimal revenue
  • Number of Exceedance: Number of days a climatic variable exceeds a threshold.
  • Vulnerability: Impact of change in climatic variables on a geography.
  • Vulnerability Reduction: Adaptation of the local society and economy to climate extremes over time.
  • Optimal Revenue: Revenues of a firm in ideal conditions without climate change exposure.

For our analysis, we have calculated vulnerability for specific locations. Selecting location-based vulnerability is beneficial for the analysis as it incorporates the relative characteristics of local weather. For example, 35°C can be very hot for people of Northern Europe but could be a normal temperature in the equatorial region. The same holds for lower temperatures. We rely on classical statistics to calculate vulnerability, making it a unitless parameter. To ensure comparability and remove the scaling effect, we have further normalized it to a range of 0 to 1. This normalization highlights outliers across different regions.
Although all climatic variables are inter-related, one often emerges as the primary driver of climate change in a specific area. Therefore, variables carrying more variability are assigned higher weights, indicating their significance. For instance, if the temperature is changing significantly, it indicates that temperature change is the primary driver of climate change in that area, mathematically.

Acute Risks:

 

Cost = annual damage × asset value + annual share of revenue lost due to business interruption × annual revenue.

Acute risks are short-term, severe impacts of climate events, such as extreme storms or floods, that can cause immediate damage to assets and disrupt operations. They are crucial to consider because they represent sudden financial shocks that can significantly affect an organization's stability and resilience.

Data Integration- Linking Climate, Location, and Financial Data:

A common question asked is about the linkage of climate data, location data, and financial data, specifically how these diverse datasets are integrated to inform comprehensive real estate analysis and decision-making. Financial Data provides specific income information, linking financial performance metrics with real estate properties. Real Estate Data then utilizes this financial information. Climate Data contributes location-specific scenarios, detailing how climate changes might impact different areas. These location-specific climate scenarios are incorporated into the Real Estate Data, providing insights into potential environmental impacts on properties. The combined data allows for comprehensive analysis and forecasting, considering both economic and environmental factors in real estate decision-making.

Figure 1 -  Linkage of Financial, Real Estate, and Climate Data for Scenario Analysis

Climate Value at Risk (CVaR):

Climate Value at Risk is a key metric for assessing the potential financial impact of climate-related risks on assets. It estimates the proportion of an asset's value that could be lost due to climate change. It is defined as the percentage ratio of the expected climate costs (or income) to the total value of the underlying asset. Using appropriate rates, the expected physical climate costs are discounted and divided by the total market value to provide an overview of the risk. This indicator, proposed by Carbon Delta, is widely used for scenario analysis as it is simple and easy to understand.

CVaR(year) = Value of climate costs or profits(year) / Market value of enterprise(year).

Conclusion
The PaPM Sample Content titled Climate Risk Scenario Analysis leverages the described methodology to allow investors to quantify the impacts of climate change on real estate by analyzing chronic and acute risks. By understanding potential vulnerabilities, investors can make informed financial decisions to mitigate climate-related risks.
Stay tuned for Part III of our blogpost series about Climate Risk Management, where we will cover the dashboard and the results of analysis.


References

  • TCFD Technical Supplement 2017: The Use of Scenario Analysis in Disclosure of Climate-Related Risks and Opportunities
  • IPCC Special Report 2000: Emissions Scenarios
    Carleton, T.A. and Hsiang, S.M., 2016. Social and economic impacts of climate. Science, 353(6304), p. aad9837.
  • UNEP FI Changing Course: A Comprehensive Investor Guide to Scenario-Based Methods for Climate Risk Assessment, in Response to the TCFD., 2019

entry.title: Save the Date! SAP returns to ADIPEC 2024
entry.content:
We’re thrilled to announce that SAP will once again be participating in ADIPEC, one of the most significant global events for the Oil, Gas, and Energy industry. Taking place at the Abu Dhabi National Exhibition Center, UAE from November 4-7, 2024, ADIPEC will host key discussions and innovations that are set to influence the future of energy. and SAP is excited to be a part of these conversations.

Our theme this year, "The Power of AI," is especially relevant as artificial intelligence continues to reshape the energy landscape. SAP is committed to leveraging AI to drive advancements across the sector, and we’ll soon be sharing our focus topics that align with this theme.

Collaboration remains central to our approach, and we’re excited to work closely with our SAP Partners again this year. We’ll be announcing our full partner lineup in the coming weeks, so stay tuned for more details. 

SAP’s booth will be located in the Digitalization Zone, Hall 14, booth no. 14265. Our Solution Experts will be onsite to discuss SAP’s latest innovations and explore crucial topics such as the evolving advancements in AI, value creation in a decarbonized world and efficient operations and asset maintenance with the latest technology innovations and breakthroughs.

We encourage you to visit us at ADIPEC 2024 to learn more about how SAP can support your energy transition journey. Register here: https://www.adipec.com/visit/

For more information, please reach out to the following:

  • Daniella Maddock daniella.maddock@sap.com
  • Manas Sahoo manas.sahoo@sap.com

.

entry.title: European Sustainability Reporting Standards(ESRS) Journey With SAP Sustainability Control Tower(SCT)
entry.content:
SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) is designed to support organizations in ESG (Environment, Social, and Governance) reporting journey by providing a set of pre-defined metrics aligned with the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS).

This blog provides a broad outline of how an organization can kick-start their European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) journey using SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) with confidence.

As part of August, 2024 release, SAP introduced "SAP Sustainability Control Tower ESRS Starter Pack" functionality with ESRS metrics.

Before we dive in, let us understand a bit about European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) and Its purpose.

The ESRS are a set of extensive standards with regard to Environment, Social, and Governance (ESG) aspects and aim to bring more transparency, accountability, and responsibility with regard to organization`s environmental and social impact. ESRS metrics have been organized into 12 extensive standards which includes, Cross-Cutting Standards, Environmental Standards, Social Standards, and Governance Standards, i.e.

  • Cross-Cutting Standards: (S1)General Requirements and (S2)General Standards
  • Environmental Standards: (E1)Climate Change, (E2)Pollution, (E3)Water and Marine Resources, (E4)Bio-Diversity and Ecosystems, (E5)Resource Use and Circular Economy.
  • Social Standards: (S1)Own Workforce, (S2)Workers In the Value Chain, (S3)Affected Communities, (S4)Consumers and End-Users
  • Governance Standards: (G1)Business Conduct

Mini Note:

Please bear in mind that the transition of ESRS journey with SAP SCT is underway and this blog highlights on how we can manage and feed the data to generate ESRS report with regard to sustainability metrics within SAP SCT using ESRS Starter Pack.

let us dive-in...

  • Data Management and Integration:

Manage Master Data: Using his application organization can import master data from CSV files, setting it apart from SAP-Provided master data. Once we import, validate and publish, the import status becomes "Published".

for instance let us look at published data for a Business Location and Energy Carrier dimensions,

Dimension - Business Location

Dimension - Energy Carrier

 Now let us configure sustainability metrics relevant for ESRS using "Manage Metrics" app.

Sustainability Metrics

 while setting up the sustainability metrics, we have to provide assurance information. 

  • Data Sourcing:

Post metric set-up, let us upload actuals using Manage ESG Data app. At this juncture, organization has got many opportunities to bring the calculate data into SCT application. We can import data either using CSV files or Import from other SAP systems , viz: SAP Datasphere, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, SAP EHS Management.

If you want to understand how organization can calculate and import GHG emissions, dive into my latest SAP EHS-Environment Management and Sustainability Control Tower blogs, they will give a broad outline of GHG emissions calculation procedure and Import strategy to be considered to import GHG emissions into Sustainability Control Tower. My blogs as follows:

  • EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-1 
  • EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-2 
  • EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-3 
  • SAP Sustainability Control Tower-Import Greenhouse Gas Emissions from SAP EHS Environment Management 
  • Addendum to SAP Sustainability Control Tower-Import Greenhouse Gas Emissions from SAP EHSM-EM 

In this blog, I have considered below mentioned measures to demonstrate ESRS report generation from SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT).

Measures

 Import Actuals:

Import Targets:

if we activate metrics for performance management, post actuals and targets import, we can view the performance of the sustainability metric.

Sustainability Metric Performance

 

  • Reporting and Analyses:

To feed the data into the report, let us navigate to ESRS Starter Pack report tile on the SAP Sustainability Control Tower launchpad.

ESRS Reporting

 

On the tab "Process Management", all the relevant activities are displayed that must be performed to get the results for all ESRS metrics and to analyze in detailed view.

For results calculation, run the activity  to read data from SCT source and calculate metrics. Now we can leverage the above shown dedicated views to access the result to report out in a table format using preconfigured layouts. We can also leverage the given options to pivot data and drill down into more details to gain additional insights. Let us access the results for our sustainability metrics by selecting option "View All Results",

then navigate to the "show data" screen,

let us use the column section to select the characteristic fields and key figure to filter the showed data.

Drill down to the ESRS standard

let us have a glance at overview tab and generate ESRS report for 2023 year.

Assign year 2023 to the report

Then system generates the ESRS Disclosures for the reporting year 2023.

ESRS Disclosures for the reporting year 2023

Now, let us spend some time to look into greater insights of ESRS Disclosures from the above report,

let us drilldown further on Climate Action aspects: Using general section of the side bar menu, input the desired values with regard to reporting year and business location, etc.

like this we can drilldown further to view the results of each ESRS standards. Now let us look at graphical representation of one of the ESRS standards.

This brings me to the end of my process walk-through. If you would like to keep up on SAP Sustainability Control Tower updates, follow me on SAP Community and LinkedIn. I hope this blog proves useful to Sustainability and Environmental Management team.

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy.C

My LinkedIn Profile: www.linkedin.com/in/pardhreddyc

entry.title: SAP at Hackfestival 2024 - Technology & Sustainability Coming Together to Find Innovative Solutions
entry.content:
The Hackfestival 2024 is a highly anticipated tech event that took place in Mannheim between September 6-8. Under the motto “Decarbonize the Planet”, the festival aimed to bring together tech enthusiasts, sustainability experts, coders, and hackers to collaborate and innovate in sustainability related hands-on challenges during a full-weekend hackathon.  

Located at Congress Center Rosengarten, it combined a competitive hackathon and free citizen track with a wide range of engaging activities for the greater public - including an exhibition area, workshops, open discussions and seminars. Participants had the opportunity to network with other tech talents and industry leaders, share their ideas, grow their career, test their skills and learn from each other. 

Apart from that, the event was also a great opportunity to bring citizens closer to sustainability in general as well as individual corporate initiatives. As an event partner, we had many valuable exchanges around SAP’s own journey to net-zero and the SAP Sustainability product portfolio at our booth. It is always meaningful to see the interest in this topic, make a contribution and provide deeper insights into corporate sustainability and thus make it more tangible for a larger audience. 

SAP team at our SAP Sustainability boothSome key facts and figures around the Hackfestival: 

  • Organized by The Hackathon Company – an independent European hackathon-as-a-service provider  
  • 300+ participants 
  • 35+ partners (Google, aws, Roche and more) 
  • 48-hours of hacking 
  • Six sustainability challenges 
  • Price pool of 10.000+ Euros 

The event was kicked-off on Friday with an opening ceremony and inspiring speech by Alexander Gerst, who talked about his journey as an ESA astronaut followed by Christian Specht, mayor of the city of Mannheim. Gunther Rothermel, co-General Manager and Chief Product Officer for SAP Sustainability, and other industry channel partners were part of a fruitful panel discussion, giving insights about the corporate view on sustainability and how technology and innovation can help take climate action and make a difference in the world.  

Gunther said: “With increasing regulatory landscapes and reporting requirements, our customers have set themselves a broad set of challenging sustainability targets...and we did the same at SAP. There is a lot we can address with software and smart approaches to embed sustainability into digital business processes...First we wanted to start by acting as an exemplar, working on our own emissions and journey to net-zero as a company...in the second step, we help our customers to become more sustainable and accompany them with our SAP Sustainability products. Imagine that SAP customers generate 87% of total global commerce....By integrating sustainability more naturally into customers transactions, we can have a huge impact on decarbonization, reduction of waste and more together. So, both these sides matter to us.”  

The panel talk has clearly shown - at SAP, we incorporate sustainability in our strategy as well as our core values and are proud to have a strong internal SAP Community that engages in voluntary projects and lives sustainability in its daily actions, because every contribution, no matter how small, can change things. 

According to this discussion, the focus of our sustainability effort is based on four key areas:  

  • Taking Climate Action and Reducing Carbon Emissions 
  • Establishing a Circular Economy to reuse material and avoid waste 
  • Integrating a Social Responsibility dimension to drive socially responsible value chains, promote respect for human rights, and create positive societal impacts.
  • Holistic Steering & Reporting: Acting on the operational layer to be compliant and safe on the production and plant level as well as in company's own transactions and processes to be transparent on the way to net-zero. 

Sustainability @SAP

Afterwards the different challenges were introduced and kicked off by the companies. The highly motivated participants had 48 hours to solve challenges provided by the “Challenge Setters” under the main topic “Decarbonize the Planet”. Divided into teams they started working on sustainability solutions, including design and business perspectives.  

Coding has started

SAP' challenge: „𝐇𝐞𝐲, 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞’𝐬 𝐜𝐚𝐫𝐛𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐦𝐲 𝐥𝐮𝐧𝐜𝐡🍴”: 

We provided the following task: “As one-third of global emissions stem from the food system, imagine the impact that can be made by addressing this challenge head-on – especially when there’s the catering needs of over 110,000 employees. ​The challenge for the participants is to develop a digital solution that helps decarbonize the planet by reducing emissions within the food system or specifically targeting food waste.” 

The SAP Challenge

Heads were put together, many innovative ideas were developed and there were no limits to creativity. In the end, we received a lot of valuable ideas, and the following team was among the winners:  

SAP challenge team: "FrostGuardians": Food waste comes from the fact that everyone wastes food every day, so with the help of AI and a camera, the team developed a fridge that manages what's in the fridge, gives tips on food that goes with a recipe and is connected to a shopping list. 

SAP team and the winnersBig congratulations to the team and all other winners for the inspiring and creative solutions. 🏅

"FrostGuardians" the proud winners of the SAP challenge

One of the main goals of the festival was to foster creativity and problem-solving skills among participants and to encourage people to develop solutions with a positive social impact. Many attendees left the festival feeling inspired and motivated to continue their work in the tech industry. 

The Hackfestival 2024 in Mannheim was a fantastic opportunity for tech enthusiasts to come together and push the boundaries of innovation. It provided a platform where like-minded individuals could collaborate and drive positive change. 

It was a pleasure to support this event as a partner. A hackathon is a great opportunity to access collective knowledge, promote creativity and team spirit, resulting in digital applications and innovative ideas that sometimes can even be the start of something big. 

There is only one thing left to say - A big thank you goes to the organizational team and all participants. Only together we can make a difference. 🌍 🌱

Please see the full list of all other winners' solutions (shared by Oliver Bruemmer, Founder & CEO The Hackathon Company):
 
Trash Buddy by Stadt Mannheim/ Metropolregion Rhein-Neckar: Scan products you want to throw away and get tips on what you can do with them. Find other solutions and don’t only throw it away. 
 
Mannheim Green City Quest by Stadt Mannheim/ Metropolregion Rhein-Neckar: Playful knowledge transfer app that encourages young people to try out new ways of thinking and exploring the city in a sustainable way with geolocations and gamification elements. 
 
Rochy - The Carbon Coach by Roche : "Rochy" gives sustainability reminders and awards points when fulfilled and proven with pictures, e.g. plate eaten empty. Collected points lead to coupons or can be donated.  
Carbon Crasher by Schwarz IT KG: Machine learning app that recommends products based on your interests, such as products that save CO2 or have a short expiration date, with special prices, coupons and gamification. 
 
KAEFER by KION Group: Efficient LLM model with key features: dynamic scoring of models, prompt model complexity matching, ML-based prompt categorization, self-learning architecture interaction.

Learn more about SAP Sustainability and subscribe to weekly Sustainability Highlight Newsletter.

entry.title: Streamlined Dangerous Goods Regulatory Updates
entry.content:
Two Ways to Receive Updates

Public Cloud customers can now receive new and updated dangerous goods compliance requirement versions (CRV) through two convenient methods:

  1. Mime Repository: For those not using our Content Service, CRV updates can be accessed directly from the Mime Repository.
  2. Regulatory Content Service: CRV updates can be loaded from the Content Service directly into your database tables, making them immediately available in your SAP system.

 Activation Still Required

This user-dependent approach offers you the flexibility to choose the method that best suits. However, using the Activate Compliance Requirements – Dangerous Goods app, you still need to review and activate the new CRV. Otherwise, the new version will be in the system, but not in use.

 Improved Efficiency

Previously, content was packaged with normal S/4HANA system updates approximately 10 times per year, with no alternative options available. This new content delivery method streamlines the process, ensuring you have access to the latest dangerous goods compliance information as soon as it becomes available.

 Future Developments

While this new content delivery method is currently available for public cloud customers, we are actively working on extending this functionality to our private cloud and on-premise customers. Stay tuned for updates found in our Roadmap, as we continue to enhance our services to better serve your needs.

 We are excited to bring you this improved content delivery system and look forward to helping you navigate the complex world of dangerous goods compliance with greater ease and efficiency.

For information on how to set-up the Regulatory Content Service for Public Cloud, see this SAP Note.

For information on how to set-up the Regulatory Content Service for Private Cloud and On-Premise, see this SAP Note.

entry.title: The Supply Chain Behind Oktoberfest: Tradition, Innovation and Sustainability
entry.content:
Oktoberfest isn’t just the largest folk festival of Bavarian culture and beer; it’s a masterclass in supply chain planning and logistics that provides an unforgettable experience for millions of visitors all around the world. With countless liters of beer flowing and thousands of meals served, this festival is a logistical marvel that showcases the power of complex planning and innovative solutions.

 

Over 7 million liters of beer were poured at Oktoberfest

At the heart of Oktoberfest is the legendary "Oktoberfest beer," brewed exclusively by six renowned breweries: Augustiner, Hacker-Pschorr, Hofbräu, Löwenbräu, Paulaner, and Spaten. These breweries don't just whip up beer overnight; they begin their production well in advance, following a traditional brewing method that has been honed over centuries.

This dedication is reflected in the extraordinary reputation of Munich's Oktoberfest, which adheres to strict quality standards, including the famous Reinheitsgebot (German Beer Purity Law). This law ensures that beer remains simple and pure by allowing only four ingredients: water, barley, hops, and yeast.

Once brewed, the beer starts on its journey to the festival grounds. Traditional wooden kegs and modern steel barrels are used for smaller-scale transportation and on-site storage, while tanker trucks transport large quantities of beer to the festival grounds.

But the real showstopper is the underground beer pipelines that transport beer directly from breweries to the festival tents. Paulaner's technical team says each beer tent is fitted with three 28,000-liter tanks connected by a 260-meter pipeline system, ensuring the beer remains fresh and cold. The automation system analyzes beer flow, temperature, and pressure, and sends hourly updates to the brewery staff via email or text regarding the beer usage from each tap station. This setup not only speeds up service but also reduces transportation costs and emissions.

 

An estimated 6 million visitors are expected to attend the festival

Oktoberfest poises to welcome 6 million visitors, who are expected to spend a staggering €400 million during the event.

To manage such a large number of visitors and ensure that supplies meet demand, organizers rely heavily on accurate forecasting. They utilize historical data and advanced analytics to predict consumption patterns throughout the festival duration. This helps in minimizing shortages and waste, ensuring that beer and food supplies are sufficient to cater to the expected crowds.

However, factors such as unexpected warm weather can significantly influence consumption rates. The weather at Oktoberfest has been as unpredictable as fall itself lately. Last year made everyone flock to the beer gardens, making seats in the festival tents hotter than a fresh pretzel out of the oven and ending up increasing beer consumption. This meant organizers had to quickly adjust their supply logistics to make sure they didn’t run out of stock.

Also, real-time digital systems play a crucial role in inventory management at the festival. Organizers can monitor beer consumption across various tents to manage stock levels effectively and ensure timely refills. This technology not only enhances operational efficiency but cuts down the wait times for drinks, allowing everyone to spend more time enjoying the event rather than waiting in line.

 

500k whole roast chickens and 60k sausages are consumed

While the beer takes center stage during the 16-day festival, the food is equally iconic, as well as the numbers. Visitors chow down on:

  • 124 oxen
  • 29 calves
  • 509,420 roast chickens
  • 122,658 pairs of pork sausages
  • 80,259 knuckles of pork and many other delicious Bavarian food.

Navigating economic pressures, regulatory requirements, and logistical hurdles is no small task for suppliers who must coordinate with local farms and distributors to deliver fresh ingredients right on time. Ensuring compliance with food safety regulations adds another layer of complexity to this already intricate operation.

 

Consumers are prompting suppliers to rethink their sourcing and waste management strategies

In recent years, Oktoberfest has prioritized sustainability within its supply chain, positioning itself as one of the most eco-friendly large-scale events worldwide. By sourcing only Munich-based breweries, the festival already minimizes transportation distances and the resulting emissions.

Many of these breweries utilize environmentally friendly packaging, including returnable bottles or kegs that can be reused up to 50 times. Additionally, soft drinks are sold solely in returnable bottles with a deposit system, while canned beverages are prohibited, significantly reducing waste generation.

Since 2000, Oktoberfest has powered all public areas with renewable energy sources. The festival utilizes green electricity and natural gas which helps offset approximately 1,000 tonnes of CO2 emissions annually.

The supply chain behind Oktoberfest is a remarkable blend of tradition, innovation, and sustainability. This festival not only highlights Bavarian traditions but also serves as a model for how large-scale events can operate sustainably, ensuring that future generations can continue to enjoy this iconic celebration. Prost to a successful Oktoberfest and greener supply chains of the future!

To discover essential strategies for developing risk-resilient and sustainable supply chains, download the Oxford Economics Report: “5 Supply Chain Strategies to Achieve Resiliency and Avoid Risk.”

entry.title: Cloud Transition for Sustainability and ESG enhancement
entry.content:
Embarking on a journey to the cloud is more than just a technological shift—it's a dynamic force reshaping our environmental impact, societal inclusivity, and governance standards. Imagine a world where our digital evolution not only propels energy efficiency, minimizes carbon footprints, and optimizes resources but also fosters a realm of accessibility, inclusivity, and robust governance. Let's dive into the transformative waves of cloud transition, exploring its ripple effects on our planet, our communities, and the ethical governance that guides our digital future.

 



Cloud Transition´s Impact on Environment


Green cloud transition significantly influences environmental aspects, focusing on energy efficiency, carbon footprint reduction, and resource optimization:

  1. Higher Energy Efficiency: Cloud transition enhances energy efficiency by optimizing data center operations, reducing energy consumption, and promoting the use of renewable energy sourcessignificantly boosting energy efficiency by up to 25%, according to Accenture studies.
  2. Reduced Carbon Footprint: By adopting green cloud practices, organizations minimize their carbon footprint through the efficient use of resources and the implementation of environmentally friendly technologies by up to 30%.
  3. Resource Optimization: Cloud transition enables better resource utilization through advanced technologies such as virtualization and containerization, leading to reduced waste and overall resource efficiency.

 

Cloud Transition's Impact on Social Responsibility


The shift to cloud computing also has a positive impact on social responsibility, fostering accessibility and inclusion for diverse user groups:

    1. Enhanced Accessibility: Cloud services facilitate universal access to computing resources, breaking down barriers and providing opportunities for users with varying needs and from different geographic locations.

 

    1. Inclusive Opportunities: Cloud platforms empower diverse user groups by providing equal access to information and services, promoting inclusivity and bridging digital divides.



Cloud Transition's Impact on Governance and Compliance


The transition to the cloud has significant implications for governance and compliance, affecting data governance, data security and operational transparency:

    1. Data Governance and Security: Cloud providers implement robust security measures, ensuring data protection and compliance with industry regulations. Organizations can benefit from advanced security features and protocols to safeguard sensitive information.

 

    1. Operational Transparency: Cloud transition enhances operational transparency through centralized management and monitoring tools. This provides organizations with a clearer view of their operations, aiding in compliance reporting and adherence to governance standards.



How to Migrate to Sustainable Green Cloud Computing?


Key steps include:

    1. Virtualization and Containerization: Reducing energy needs through dividing hardware into portions and promoting virtual infrastructure.

 

    1. Micro Data Centres: Lightweight, movable data centers that lower reaction time and decrease downtime.

 

    1. Adopting Rank-Based Microservices: Moving to microservices-based architecture and using containers for reduced energy consumption.

 

    1. AI-based Resource Scheduling: Efficient resource scheduling using AI to intelligently allocate resources and minimize power consumption.

 

    1. Effective Infrastructure Utilization with IaaS: Delegating infrastructure management to cloud providers for intelligent use of virtual machines and containers.



 



Conclusion


Cloud transition not only contributes to environmental sustainability by optimizing energy use and reducing carbon footprints but also aligns with social responsibility by promoting accessibility and inclusion. Additionally, the impact on governance and compliance is significant, with enhanced data security and operational transparency ensuring that organizations meet regulatory standards and ethical considerations in their cloud journey.

 

Joaquín Carballo

Sustainability Principal

M +54 9 11 2311 6947 | LinkedIn

entry.title: 3rd Latin America Executive Value Network (EVN) for Water and Waste Water Services companies
entry.content:
Current edition of the Executive Value Network (EVN) pursues the consolidation to what is an regional knowledge sharing forum focused into the analysis and discussion of digital transformation use cases of Water and Waste Water companies with the purpose to get valuable insights from peers and explore how to improve inefficient traditional processes while embracing new relevant technologies. 

 Image: EVN previous meetings in Bucaramanga (2022) and Cartagena de Indias (2023)

​Success of previous years EVN editions have motivated to meet this year in Barranquilla, hosted by Triple A which is the local water company.  Latin America EVN also has inspired other territories of the region to explore digital transformation cases in the water sector and network among peers and last may  there was a similar event in Mexico, hosted by hosted by the Servicios de Agua y Drenaje de Monterrey (Mexico)  where you can find a summary here 

 

​Sessions are designed to bring  inspirational use cases to open a discusión among participants. For current edition tracks Will be

  • How an intelligent water management platform assure water and waste water services companies resilience to adress corrent and future challenges.
  • Developing a Customer first culture:  purpose, benefits and challenges. How its application impacts corporate IT landscape.
  • Technical asset Management as a discipline:  which are the benefits of efficiency? How Innovation are trasforming field work and investment strategies
  • How a data driven policy assures transparency, resource optimization and intelligent decisions

Sessions will be complemented with a site visit to Barranquilla's new Marllorquin Swamp Ecopark 

 

​Thanks to our partners  Misait and Smart Energy and Water – SEW (links), the Executive Networking Dinner help attendees to join an executive community that extends beyond the conference, gaining knowledge from peers and other companies, partners and vendors.

​For those interested to join and take part in our discussions we have set up a registration website where updates on agenda and other useful information is as well  https://events.sap.com/lac/evn-agua-alcantarillado-lac-north-2024/es/home

​Looking forward to see you in Barranquilla!

entry.title: Sustainability in Defense and Security: Why It’s Essential
entry.content:
Why Sustainability Matters

 

“We simply must do everything we can in our power to slow down global warming before it is too late. We can save our planet and also boost our economy at the same time.”

 

You may now be wondering what this has to do with Defense and Security organizations such as armed forces, firefighters, police, ambulance services, or aid organizations. The answer is simply more than most people think. For one thing, according to the leading consultancy McKinsey, D&S organizations account for approximately 50% of governments’ carbon emissions from fleets and facilities in countries like Canada and the United Kingdom, and up to about 80% in the United States of America. On the other hand, if you consider global CO2 emissions, the military sector accounts for 1-5%. Although this stake does not seem high at first glance, it certainly does when you set it in relation to other business sectors and see that the emissions are comparably high as in the aviation or shipping industry. This means that any government that aims to reduce its overall emissions needs to consider its defense forces in its climate action plan too. For another thing, current, state-of-the-art sustainability solutions do not really harm the organizational performance or the practical operational readiness of military organizations anymore. This is especially because key processes, such as the measurement of emissions, are usually run in the background through automated calculations and the power of highly innovative sensor technology and software. But more on that later. Before that, I want to inform you about some additional statistical data and facts about sustainability. The consulting company Boston Consulting Group (BCG) found that the global defense industry’s share in global CO2 emissions could rise significantly from 2% in 2021 to 25% in 2050 if the industry does not take active action to reduce its emissions. But what do these emissions look like in detail?

 

Figure 1: CO2 Emissions of the US Department of Defense (2023)

 

As the above pie chart of the CO2 emissions of the US Department of Defense in 2023 shows, most emissions result from vehicles and equipment or purchased electricity. In Germany, however, where the emissions of the Bundeswehr increased by 17.9% in the period of 2019 to 2021, we see a somewhat different picture since most of the increase results from increased fuel consumption of the air force as well as increased heating requirements of the Bundeswehr’s military infrastructure. Interestingly, as the well-known Nature magazine pointed out in an article, many military organizations around the world still do not measure and report their emissions in as much detail as other governmental organizations or private companies do. In contrast, 95% of the largest companies globally publish ESG (environmental, social, and governance) reports regularly.

 

What Actions are Required to meet the Climate Targets?

 

However, although the above statistics may not look really promising to the reader, we luckily see that the problem was recognized a long time ago by many Defense & Security organizations. Military organizations such as the US Department of Defense, the German Bundeswehr, or NATO have actively created climate action plans within the last few years:

 

The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) in this regard set itself the following targets in 2021:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 45% by 2030.
  • Achievement of net zero emissions (Net zero emissions occur when the amount of greenhouse gases emitted equals the amount removed from the atmosphere) by 2050.

 

The European Union, on the other hand, has set the defense sector within the Union similar climate action goals:

  • Reduction of greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55% by 2030 compared to 1990 levels.
  • Carbon neutrality by 2050.
  • Increased use of renewable energies and focus on improvement of energy efficiency within military operations.
  • Implementation of sustainable practices in defense operations.
  • Passing of a so-called “Climate-Security Nexus”, that addresses the link between climate change and security threats, thereby including climate considerations into defense planning and operations.

 

As can be seen in the text above, there are already a number of promising strategic plans, that include proven concepts and best practices for reducing greenhouse gas emissions of military organizations. However, specialized management consultancies such as BCG and McKinsey in this regard have drawn up a number of additional recommendations for making defense organizations more sustainable:

 

Recommendations from McKinsey

McKinsey suggests leveraging low-carbon technologies by using electric vehicles, sustainable aviation fuel, and renewable energy sources. Additionally, the consultancy recommends putting the focus mainly on non-mission-critical areas. This according to McKinsey allows armed forces organizations to achieve a 60% reduction in Scope 1 and 2 emissions. Other suggestions from the company include to establish an end-to-end consideration of sustainability in value chains and supply chains of Defense and Security organizations.

 

Recommendations from the Boston Consulting Group:

The US-based consulting company Boston Consulting Company (BCG) principally makes similar suggestions. It’s main recommendation is to make significant investments into sustainable technologies through research and development. Furthermore, it highly recommends D&S organizations to enhance emissions reporting and accountability as well as to pursue net-zero emission goals.

 

Other Recommendations and Initiatives:

The consulting company KPMG suggests D&S organizations to align their climate goals and actions with international and national climate targets, whereas Nature advocates for mandatory emissions reporting and international agreements and methodologies to monitor and reduce military emissions. Additionally, D&S organizations such as the US DoD or the US Army have established their own guidelines for water efficiency and water security.

 

Sustainability Targets of Emergency Organizations:

These recommendations are not only true for military organizations, but are applicable to emergency organizations like Police, Ambulances or Firefighting organizations in a similar manner. Examples include organizations such as the London Fire Brigade, which has outlined a “Sustainable Development Strategy for 2023-2025” and consequently invested in measures like acquiring electric vehicles and making their fire stations more energy efficient. Other examples in this direction include the Berlin Police, that has implemented measures such as using hybrid and electric cars along with sustainable practices such as a focus on recycling and a reduction of paper use. In doing so the emergency organization aims to support the City of Berlin in reaching its climate reduction 2030 and climate neutrality 2045 goals.

 

Given the above-mentioned challenges and facts, I now want to draw the reader’s attention to the holistic SAP Sustainability Solutions, that allow D&S organizations to actively tackle and manage all common aspects of sustainability.  

 

The SAP Sustainability Solutions

 

 

Video 1: ERP to ESG: How to develop your first ESG Strategy

 

SAP is proud to be one of the leading software suppliers for Enterprise Resource Planning and other software solutions for various leading companies and organizations around the world. However, what is less well-known across customers and industries is that SAP also offers powerful and holistic cross-industry sustainability software solutions. The leading technology research companies, Gartner and IDC, have recognized SAP’s sustainability solutions for their comprehensive capabilities. Gartner has rated SAP as a preferred vendor for carbon accounting and management software solutions, while IDC has praised the “breadth and depth” of SAP’s offerings. The SAP solutions are thereby not only highly capable of measuring, analyzing, and managing carbon emissions, but are also trusted by leading companies and organizations worldwide, such as IBM, PWC, Freudenberg, msg global, and SAP itself. SAP’s sustainability solutions feature real-time data analytics, seamless integration with existing ERP systems, and AI-driven insights to help businesses make informed decisions. For example, IBM has leveraged SAP’s solutions to significantly reduce its carbon footprint, while PWC has used them to enhance its sustainability reporting. SAP’s commitment to sustainability has also been recognized with several industry awards, and the company continues to innovate with new features and developments on the horizon.

In general, the SAP Sustainability Cloud software suite is built on the following four main software solutions:

 

1) Sustainability and ESG Reporting Software 

 

Video 2: SAP Sustainability Control Tower – Holistic Steering and ESG Reporting

 

Figure 2: The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution

 

SAP allows companies and organizations to connect their environmental, social, and governance (ESG) data and steer their organizations towards more sustainable and efficient operations, thereby also helping to achieve national and international climate goals. The SAP ESG software was designed in a holistic way and provides streamlined reporting as well as valuable insights for decision-makers and stakeholders. Furthermore, it allows for the full integration of the organization’s stakeholders, like suppliers, into the system. This also means that governments theoretically can implement the solution nation-wide or across different governmental areas for managing everything around sustainability with one central software platform. Apart from that, the software generally allows D&S organizations to measure, communicate, and achieve corporate sustainability goals to the broader public. Moreover, the sustainability software is integrable across various different systems, even across the respective organization or company. This allows bringing sustainability, safety, risk, compliance, asset, and people data together in one place for enabling comprehensive sustainability reporting and analysis.

 

2) SAP Climate Action Solutions

 

Video 3: Transactional Carbon Accounting with SAP

 

Figure 3: Measuring emissions with the SAP Sustainability Management solutions

 

The SAP Climate Action Solutions allow companies and organizations to decarbonize their entire value chain from end-to-end to become climate-neutral organizations. This is made possible by integrating actual business transactions with carbon accounting software, emissions and compliance management, carbon footprint calculations, e-mobility, and transportation logistics. The solution includes services like:

  • Real-time decision making with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management
  • SAP Sustainability Data Exchange
  • SAP Environment, Health and Safety Management
  • and many more services

 

3) Circular Economy Software 

 

Video 4: Circular Design with SAP

 

Figure 4: The SAP Circular Economy solutions

 

The SAP Circular Economy solutions allow companies and organizations to integrate the concept of circularity into their organization. Circularity thereby allows them to reduce the use of resources and materials, such as plastic, as much as possible. In addition, the concept aims to eliminate waste and circulate materials. Our Circular Economy solutions thereby consider all these requirements and allow managing circularity holistically. This includes circularity in supply chains, production, and all other applicable organizational processes. Especially worth mentioning in this regard is the possibility to use blockchain technology for capturing the whole material provenance from raw materials to finished products. Other promising features of the circular economy solutions are, for instance, the ability to create circular logistic flows for transport containers, pallets, and other forms of packaging.

 

4) Social Responsibility Solutions

 

Video 5: Social Responsibility for Zero Inequality with SAP

 

Figure 5: The EcoVadis API Connector for SAP Ariba

 

The SAP Social Responsibility solutions allow companies and organizations to manage their impact on people and society centrally with one software package. This involves the ability to consider social and environmental requirements across the entire value and supply chains of your organization. Moreover, it encompasses the ability to proactively ensure health, safety, as well as labor rights within the entire organization, but also to establish workforce sustainability by creating equal treatment and opportunities like, for instance, guaranteeing equal pay for men and women. In addition, SAP within this thematic area offers D&S organizations a human rights due diligence software that allows them to enforce human rights across all supply chains.

 

Artificial Intelligence Functions and Features

 

SAP is planning to integrate Artificial Intelligence into all eligible software solutions. For instance, it has set a goal to embed its Generative AI-powered Joule Copilot into 80% of the most used tasks by the end of 2024. However, various super-helpful AI solutions are already available, including those for SAP Sustainability Management solutions, where our customers can benefit from:

 

- AI for Emission Factor Management

(Demo Video: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Features)

Our customers can simplify emission factor management by using the SAP Business AI-powered automation function, which is directly integrated into the solution. This improves result accuracy and reduces manual labor.

 

- AI for ESG Report Generation

The SAP Sustainability Control Tower solution allows users to record, report, and act on their diverse sustainability data. SAP has recently enhanced this solution by integrating an AI feature for the automated creation of ESG reports. This streamlines the reporting process, saving valuable time and money.

 

 

The Security Architecture of the SAP Sustainability Management Solutions

 

The SAP Sustainability Management software suite is built on an end-to-end security architecture that meets the demands of even the most security-conscious companies and organizations around the world. This includes:

 

- Cloud-Based Security

The sustainability software solutions are built with a security-first cloud approach. The cloud solution is based on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), which includes strict functionalities for access control and data encryption. Additionally, SAP continuously develops its products and distributes regular software updates and upgrades to its clients.

 

- Data Integration and Auditing

SAP Sustainability Management is directly integrated or integrable into core Enterprise Resource Planning processes, making all data directly traceable and auditable.

 

- AI-Enabled Automation

Artificial Intelligence technology plays an important role through the SAP Joule Copilot and various embedded AI functions within SAP applications. It is also crucial for the security of the SAP Sustainability Management solutions. AI is used for process automation, eliminating the risk of human error, and for continuous monitoring of data and processes. If the system detects any security threat, it will directly inform the system user, who can then investigate and resolve the issue.

 

- Flexible and Extensible Architecture

SAP Sustainability Control Tower, one of the core sustainability solutions, offers clients maximum flexibility by allowing them to integrate custom content and create customized solutions.

 

- Integration of Regulatory Standards for Compliance

The SAP Sustainability Management solutions allow the incorporation of all major regulatory frameworks and legal requirements into the system. Examples for this include for instance the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). Defense and Security organizations can also integrate their own sustainability guidelines, such as national and international standards or guidelines from defense organizations like NATO.

 

 

Conclusion

 

In conclusion, this blog entry has shown that sustainability is crucial for Defense and Security (D&S) organizations, not only for achieving organizational, national, or international climate goals but also for creating significant opportunities for organizational realignment towards a greener orientation. By adopting sustainable practices, D&S organizations can contribute to combating global warming while simultaneously saving significant costs on fuel consumption, heating systems, and weapon systems, without sacrificing their core defense capabilities. It is important to note the significant positive influence such transformations can have on public perception and the innovative strength of individual military organizations and the entire D&S industry. A good example is the automotive industry, which, like many other industries, has undergone significant transformation in recent years with the shift to electric drives, now offering more modern and technologically advanced products, that are also more environmentally friendly. António Guterres, the Secretary-General of the United Nations, aptly stated, “The climate emergency is a race we are losing, but it is a race we can win.” This call to action resonates deeply within the defense sector, urging all stakeholders to embrace sustainable practices and technologies. However, setting climate goals and creating corresponding guidelines is one thing, but achieving them another. That is why D&S organizations need to develop holistic sustainability concepts in partnership with key stakeholders, such as governments, suppliers, defense organizations, or supranational organizations like the European Union. They also need to measure, manage, and report their progress. This is where SAP’s cutting-edge SAP Sustainability Management solutions come into play. The software package covers all typical sustainability requirements for D&S organizations and includes various supplementary tools and features, such as ESG reporting, circular economy solutions, social responsibility, health and safety solutions, and innovative AI solutions. In conclusion, by establishing climate and sustainability action plans and utilizing powerful sustainability management solutions like SAP Sustainability Management, D&S organizations can set a powerful example, proving that security and sustainability can go hand in hand. Let’s embark on this journey together towards a greener, more sustainable future for everyone.

 

 

Sources

 

Berlin Energy and Climate Protection Programme 2030. (2018, January, 25). Berlin.de.

Climate change considerations for EU. (2022, June). European Parliament.

Climate-Neutral Berlin 2045. (2021, August). Berlin.de.

Explore the SAP Sustainability Solutions. (2024, May, 14). SAP Community.

SAP Sustainability Control Tower Update: Holistic ESG . (2022, November). SAP News.
